coaching.title: Today's Run
coaching.message: Easy 8–10 miles, Z2 only (120–135 bpm), no vert work. You're 75 days out in taper week 23, and your resting HR is 4 bpm elevated from your 30-day average—a sign CNS fatigue is still present despite decent recovery score. Your last three runs (12.05mi@139, 12.01mi@144, 8.03mi@131) averaged mid-Z3; that's appropriate for peak phase, but taper means backing off intensity completely now. Sunday is your second long effort window, but in taper, this is a shakeout day. Keep it conversational, sub-140 bpm, and if you have travel tomorrow for work, keep it short and easy—75 days is enough time to stay sharp without grinding.

coaching.title: Fuel Right
coaching.message: You slept 8.55h with 92.5% efficiency—excellent. Capitalize on that recovery by eating a full breakfast with 80–100g carbs, 25–30g protein, and healthy fat within 90 minutes of waking. During today's easy run, sip water only unless you exceed 90 minutes, then carry a single gel or bottle of sports drink. Post-run, refuel within 30 minutes with 30–40g carbs + 15–20g protein to lock in glycogen before travel (if applicable). Taper doesn't mean eating less—it means eating smart to support CNS and glycogen resynthesis while reducing training load.

coaching.title: Recovery
coaching.message: Your elevated resting HR and slight recovery dip suggest lingering fatigue, not illness. Prioritize 8+ hours again tonight—you've proven you can hit 8.5h consistently, so repeat that. Avoid caffeine after 2 PM, especially if you're traveling tomorrow. Consider 5–10 minutes of easy mobility or foam rolling post-run, but skip intense stretching or additional stressors. Your HRV data is missing, so you can't micro-manage based on autonomic tone, but consistent sleep and taper volume will lower that RHR back to 60–61 by race week. Hydration: drink to pale urine color throughout the day, not just around the run.

coaching.title: Mental Edge
coaching.message: Taper anxiety is real at 75 days out—you've built a solid block and now you're pulling back, which feels counterintuitive. That elevated resting HR is partly nervous system fatigue, partly taper fidget. Commit to the plan: your peak block (wk 16–19) delivered the vert and pace work; now your job is sleep, easy runs, and trust. Review one section of the Julian Alps 50K route mentally—elevation profile, aid station locations, technical descents—but don't obsess. You're a 40-something executive who runs 6 days a week; you've already proved discipline. Use these 75 days to stay fresh, not to cram.
